original_abstract:
Modern public policymaking is increasingly shaped by the opportunity to leverage vast "big data" streams—from e-services and mobile apps to social media—into evidence-based decisions. While Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) provide the computational power necessary to automate and scale the analysis of these datasets, their integration into the public sector is fraught with technical, political, and operational hurdles. Crucially, AI-driven policies must not only be accurate but also transparent and explainable to be trusted by officials and the public, all while complying with strict new regulatory frameworks like the European Union’s AI Act.

This paper addresses these challenges by introducing a comprehensive technological ecosystem designed for trustworthy policymaking. Our contributions include: (i) a reference architecture specifically designed for AI-based policy development; (ii) a virtualized, cloud-based platform—the Virtual Policy Management Environment (VPME)—that facilitates the collaborative specification and implementation of data-driven policies; (iii) a machine learning framework capable of generating transparent, rule-based explanations for "black box" models; and (iv) practical guidelines for aligning these technical solutions with legal regulatory requirements. We conclude by demonstrating the real-world utility of these tools through various pilot cases involving local government operations, such as smart parking and infrastructure management.
humanized_abstract:
Big data, e-services, mobile apps, social media and all new digital sources of information open numerous opportunities for decision makers to gain insights and prove policies. Big data analyses is one of the domains where Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning have the potential to facilitate and automate the activities related to processing the large quantities of information in order to achieve faster and better policy making. However, also here we have to do with numerous technological, political, administrative barriers to implement the AI/ML innovations. Furthermore, not only accuracy is expected but also policies implemented with AI and ML tools need also to be explained and explained in a way which is acceptable also for politicians, officials and citizens. So they need to be also transparent and all while having to observe the new norms (regulations) around AI like the European AI Act.

AI4Policy — Toward a Technology Ecosystem for Trustworthy Policy-Making In this paper we tackle some of the existing challenges and obstacles in the process of policy-making by proposing a technology ecosystem towards trustworthy policy-making. Our contributions are the following: A reference architecture for the support of policy-making activities using Artificial Intelligence techniques. A virtual infrastructure — the Virtual Policy Management Environment (VPME) — conceived in the cloud infrastructure paradigm and aiming at facilitating the collective process of defining and implementing data-driven policies in a collaborative manner. A machine learning approach for explaining how black-box models were derived, with the aid of transparent and human-interpretable if-then-else style production rules. Recommendations on how each of the proposed technological innovations can be made compliant with applicable legislation. Finally, we present and discuss examples of potential use cases for the technologies we propose by carrying out a series of experiments on municipal administration that aim at increasing efficiency through the use of smart parking technologies and intelligent infrastructure.
